Query: Can third trimester ultrasound predict the presentation of the first twin at delivery?

Options:
A: yes
B: no
C: maybe

Document [0] (Title: Antepartum Care in the Second and Third Trimester -- Function -- Second- and Third-Trimester Imaging Studies and Antenatal Fetal Surveillance) Additionally, in patients who are identified as having high-risk pregnancies or clinical suspicion of a pregnancy complication (eg, fundal height less than gestational age or vaginal bleeding), ultrasound imaging may be performed for diagnostic evaluation or as part of antenatal surveillance. Indications for additional ultrasound evaluation in the second or third trimesters include a vast array of issues that necessitate assessment of fetal growth and well-being, amniotic fluid levels, and/or placental location and bleeding. Typically, fetal growth assessments require serial ultrasounds performed at 4-week intervals. [19] See StatPearls' companion references, " Fetal Growth Restriction " and " Sonography 3rd Trimester and Placenta Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation ," for further information.
Document [1] (Title: Antepartum Care in the Second and Third Trimester -- Introduction) Antepartum care, or prenatal care, is the health care provided during pregnancy to optimize outcomes for both the mother and the fetus. The primary objectives are to identify high-risk pregnancies and monitor the health of the mother and the development of the fetus. Following an initial visit that ideally occurs in the first trimester, regular follow-up visits are scheduled every 4 weeks in the second trimester (ie, from 14 to 28 weeks gestation) and more frequently in the third trimester (ie, from 28 weeks to delivery), increasing to every 2 weeks from 28 to 36 weeks gestation, then every week from 36 weeks until birth. [1] High-risk pregnancies necessitate more frequent surveillance. Antepartum care in the second and third trimester monitors a wide array of issues during the pregnancy course, involving ongoing clinical assessment, conducting various laboratory and imaging studies, patient counseling, and preparation for delivery. Prenatal care has become the most frequently utilized healthcare service within the United States (US), with 98% of women who give birth initiating regular care at some point in their pregnancy. [2] After the first positive pregnancy test, care is typically sought by patients and begins after a confirmed sonographic intrauterine pregnancy. In the US, the average number of visits ranges between 12 and 14, depending on the complexity of the pregnancy course. However, the World Health Organization (WHO) has recommended a minimum of 8 visits for low-risk patients. [1]
Document [2] (Title: Sonography 1st Trimester Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Introduction) Pregnant women will typically have at least one ultrasound during their pregnancies. If only one ultrasound can be performed during pregnancy, it should be performed in the mid-second trimester (18 to 22 weeks) to detect fetal anomalies and growth abnormalities. [3] [4] First trimester ultrasounds are not a requirement for all pregnancies to confirm viability. [5] However, if any high-risk features (advanced maternal age, twin pregnancy, etc.) or clinical concerns are present (irregular menses, vaginal bleeding, etc.), more than one ultrasound may be performed at the clinician's discretion, with the first ideally in the late first trimester between 10+0 and 13+6 weeks. [5] However, ultrasound exams before ten weeks are common and can still be used as accurate assessments for most necessary clinical determinations. [6]
Document [3] (Title: Antepartum Care in the Second and Third Trimester -- Function -- Second- and Third-Trimester Imaging Studies and Antenatal Fetal Surveillance) Because the second trimester encompasses a vast majority of the rapid fetal growth period, ultrasound surveys and antenatal fetal surveillance are performed during this trimester to provide optimal time for potential interventions. A standard fetal anatomy transabdominal ultrasound examination is recommended at 18 to 20 weeks gestation, as this is the optimal time to visualize fetal anatomic structures. This fetal imaging provides a thorough assessment of the developing pregnancy, including placental location, amniotic fluid volume, fetal number, size, presentation, cardiac activity, and anatomy. The cervix should also be assessed during this ultrasound examination for any abnormalities (eg, shortening or funneling). [19] See StatPearls' companion references, " Cervical Insufficiency " and " Sonography 2nd Trimester Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation ," for more information.
Document [4] (Title: Sonography Fetal Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Indications -- Specialized Third Trimester Ultrasound Examination) Third-trimester ultrasounds are indication-driven examinations. Examples of types of specialized ultrasounds include the biophysical profile, additional biometric measurements follow-ups (every 3 to 4 weeks), doppler ultrasounds in the setting of growth restrictions. Indications include fetal growth restriction or suspected macrosomia, multifetal gestation, twin-twin transfusion syndrome screening (every two weeks), a significant discrepancy between the uterine size and clinical dates, evaluation of fetal well-being, evaluation of prelabour rupture of membranes, preterm labor, vaginal bleeding, suspected placental abruption, follow-up evaluation for placental appearance for suspected placenta previa, vasa previa, PAS, suspected fetal death, suspected amniotic fluid abnormalities. [1] [12]
Document [5] (Title: Sonography 3rd Trimester and Placenta Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Introduction) Sonographic assessments in the third trimester are not routinely performed unless the patient has had no initial sonographic examination or prior pathological or concerning conditions are identified during the first or second trimesters. [1]
Document [6] (Title: Sonography 3rd Trimester and Placenta Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Clinical Significance) A commonly asked question during the ultrasonographic examination is the presence of fetal abnormalities. It is impractical to examine every patient for every possible abnormality. However, major abnormalities are usually evident on a routine examination. Determining what is considered reassuring in the absence of any obvious abnormality remains debated, given the complexity of the issue. Furthermore, abnormalities detected during the first and second trimester of pregnancy can disappear in the third trimester. Opposite to that, some abnormalities only become apparent with the advancement of gestation. It is currently recommended that if only one ultrasound is performed, that it should be done at 18 to 20 weeks of gestation. Any malformation found should be further evaluated by a sonographer with expertise in the precise abnormality discovered.
Document [7] (Title: Sonography 2nd Trimester Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes) A well-defined strategy involves establishing standardized protocols and guidelines for conducting second-trimester obstetric sonography. This ensures consistency in care delivery and reduces variations in practice. It also includes setting objectives for the scan, such as screening for fetal anomalies or assessing fetal growth based on the patient's specific clinical indications.
Document [8] (Title: Sonography 1st Trimester Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Indications) First-trimester ultrasonography may be performed for a variety of reasons. Typical indications include pelvic pain, suspected ectopic pregnancy, suspected twin pregnancy, vaginal bleeding, suspected trophoblastic disease, assessment for fetal growth abnormalities, measurement of nuchal translucency, evaluation of pelvic masses or uterine abnormalities, and as an adjunct to chorionic villus sampling. [6] [12] Asymptomatic patients in the first trimester may also be scheduled for a routine ultrasound when resources are available to establish an accurate gestational age. [4]

Document [10] (Title: Sonography 3rd Trimester and Placenta Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Clinical Significance) Fetal lie and presentation are of higher importance in the third trimester than the first and second trimester. Change in these two parameters decreased as the due date approaches. Fetal lie refers to the angle of alignment of the fetus and uterus in the sagittal axis. Longitudinal lie, the most commonly found, refers to both the fetal and uterine axis aligning in parallel. The other two possibilities include a transverse, and less commonly, an oblique lie. [12]
Document [11] (Title: Sonography 2nd Trimester Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Introduction) The detection rate of the screening second-trimester ultrasound scan is good in high-risk patients examined by a well-trained sonographer. On the other hand, the scan can also be sensitive in anomaly detection if a systemic searching pattern is followed in low-risk pregnant women. [7]
Document [12] (Title: Sonography 3rd Trimester and Placenta Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Contraindications) Transvaginal ultrasound is relatively contraindicated during the third trimester, especially in patients without a prior documented placental location, due to concerns for precipitating bleeding in the setting of undiagnosed placenta previa. It is recommended to begin with a transabdominal ultrasound to locate the placenta first, followed by the transvaginal ultrasound if clinically indicated and necessary. However, due to the cervix and the vaginal canal angle, performing transvaginal ultrasound during the third trimester can be potentially safe. The risk of precipitating clinically significant hemorrhage has not been consistently supported in the literature. [5]
Document [13] (Title: Sonography 1st Trimester Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Anatomy and Physiology) The first sign of intrauterine pregnancy is the gestational sac, an anechoic structure that appears between the fourth and fifth gestational weeks but is small at 2 or 3 mm in the greatest dimension. [8] Although not always seen, the double decidual sign is the first widely accepted sign of intrauterine pregnancy. [8] [9] It is composed of two echogenic rings, the decidua capsularis (inner ring) and decidua vera (outer ring), which surround the developing gestational sac. [10] [8] Because the double decidual sac sign may be confused with a pseudogestational sac (nonviable), many clinicians elect to visualize a yolk sac before confirming an intrauterine pregnancy. The yolk sac is typically visible by ultrasound in the fifth week and appears as a hyperechoic ring with an anechoic center located within the gestational sac. [8] [11]

Document [16] (Title: Antepartum Care in the Second and Third Trimester -- Function -- Second-Trimester Laboratory Studies) Several laboratory and aneuploid screening studies are performed during the second trimester also to provide optimal time for potential interventions. In patients who have elected to perform combined first- and second-trimester aneuploidy screening tests, an independent second-trimester serum screening (eg, quad screen) or amniocentesis for diagnostic aneuploidy testing, the specimen should be collected at the appropriate time. [14] [15] See StatPearls' companion reference, " Prenatal Genetic Screening ," for more information on various aneuploidy screening tests. ACOG and USPSTF also recommend screening laboratory studies for gestational diabetes and anemia be performed in all pregnant individuals between 24 to 28 weeks gestation. The most common method for gestational diabetes screening is the 1-hour 50-g glucose tolerance screening, though some institutions use a 75-g, 2-hour glucose tolerance diagnostic test. Different cut-off thresholds are used for the 50-g glucose tolerance screening to be considered an abnormal result, including ≥135 mg/dL (7.5 mmol/L), ≥130 mg/dL (7.22 mmol/L), and ≥140 mg/dL (7.8 mmol/L). Because studies have not demonstrated an optimal cut-off threshold, clinicians should determine which cut-off to implement based on the prevalence of community gestational diabetes risk factors and clinical preference for test sensitivity and specificity. In patients with a positive 50-g glucose screen, a diagnostic test is necessary with a 100-g, 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test. Document [18] (Title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Evaluation) In the first trimester of pregnancy, ultrasound evaluation is imperative to diagnose the chorionicity of the twin pregnancy. With no dividing amniotic membrane between the twins, monoamnionicity is directly diagnosed. Indirect signs may be entangling of the umbilical cords. [2]
Document [19] (Title: Antepartum Care in the Second and Third Trimester -- Function -- Second- and Third-Trimester Imaging Studies and Antenatal Fetal Surveillance) Antenatal fetal surveillance is primarily indicated in high-risk pregnant patients or those with abnormal clinical findings (eg, decreased fetal movement) suggestive of an increased risk for fetal demise. [9] Antenatal fetal surveillance comprises several techniques, including kick counts, nonstress tests, contraction stress tests, biophysical profiles, and umbilical artery blood flow velocity. Clinicians must determine which testing strategy to employ, the testing frequency, and when to initiate antenatal fetal surveillance based on various clinical factors (eg, complication severity, survival prognosis, and gestational age). ACOG recommends antenatal fetal surveillance not be initiated until the patient reaches a gestational age at which fetal demise is more likely and the clinician is willing to deliver; typically suggesting 32 weeks gestation to be appropriate for most high-risk patients. Moreover, 32 weeks gestation is the age most fetuses become reactive on nonstress testing. However, clinicians may choose to initiate testing earlier due to clinical risk factors. Antenatal fetal surveillance is commonly performed at weekly to twice weekly intervals; however, the optimal frequency has not been defined, as this must be individualized according to the clinical picture. [9] See StatPearls' companion references, " Prenatal Nonstress Test ," " Ultrasound Biophysical Profile ," " Fetal Growth Restriction ," and " Sonography 3rd Trimester and Placenta Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation , for more detailed information regarding these evaluation studies.
Document [20] (Title: Sonography 3rd Trimester and Placenta Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes) The approach to taking care of the pregnant patient during the third trimester of pregnancy requires an interdisciplinary effort involving physicians, nurses, midwives, pharmacists, and other healthcare team members. There are multiple factors to consider during this period, and ultrasound has evolved as an essential tool to evaluate the progression of pregnancy. Even though there is no universal recommendation to obtain a routine ultrasound in the third trimester, its use can help identify pathology that can directly affect the outcome of both the patient and the pregnancy. Understanding the applications and limitations of this tool can help reduce maternal and neonatal morbidity and mortality.
Document [21] (Title: Antepartum Care in the Second and Third Trimester -- Function -- Prenatal Management in the Second Trimester) Fetal heart rates can be detected via Doppler ultrasound in nonobese patients at as early as 10 weeks gestation. At approximately 20 weeks gestation, patients may begin to perceive fetal movement, also referred to as "quickening". Clinicians should inquire about fetal movement at each visit after a patient reports feeling fetal movement. [10] During this trimester, patients are also counseled after fetal viability to be aware of fetal movement and to report perceived decreased fetal movement; some clinicians may use various "fetal kick count" methods to quantify fetal movement (eg, 10 movements within 2 hours), though the optimal protocol has not been defined. [13] Abdominal palpation to assess fundal height at each visit after 20 weeks gestation and fetal presentation at 36 weeks gestation should also be performed. [10] See StatPearls' companion reference, " Leopold Maneuvers ," for more information on fetal presentation assessment through palpation.
Document [22] (Title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Evaluation) Obstetric ultrasound evaluations for twin gestations depend on determining the chorionicity of the gestation. An ultrasound at 16 weeks for a fetal anatomy survey is appropriate in dizygotic twin gestations. Subsequently, it should be done every 3 to 4 weeks for fetal growth and confirmation of fetal anatomy. [16] Multiple fetal anatomy evaluations minimize the risk of missing a fetal anomaly and also allow diagnosis of abnormalities that may not present or be available for early diagnosis. Dizygotic twins have an additive risk for congenital anomalies. In monozygotic twins, there is an exaggerated risk for midline anomalies and cardiac anomalies that merit routine fetal echocardiography at 22 weeks. All twin gestations are also at an increased risk for abnormal placentation (placenta previa) and velamentous cord insertions with its attendant risk for vasa previa. Transvaginal scan at 18 weeks with color Doppler mapping allows detection of vasa previa. Intrapartum rupture of the second sac also entails the risk of rupturing an unappreciated gestational vessel; thus, the wisdom for seeking recognition of any pulsation in the membranes before their rupture. [17]
Document [23] (Title: Sonography 3rd Trimester and Placenta Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Introduction) The use of ultrasound in the third trimester of pregnancy serves a multitude of general and specialized purposes that include but are not limited to the determination of fetal number and presentation, assessment of growth disorders, and characterization of the placenta and amniotic fluid. Thus, the ultrasonographic applications in the third trimester of pregnancy differ from previous trimesters in both scope and objectives.
Document [24] (Title: Sonography Assessment of Gestational Age -- Technique or Treatment -- Second Trimester) Changes in growth, genetic variations, and differences in attributes of the growing fetus make the determination of gestational age within the second and third trimesters less accurate.  Using multiple methods appears to be more accurate than using a single measurement. There are five methods commonly used during these stages of development. [4] [11] [12]
Document [25] (Title: Sonography 2nd Trimester Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Indications) An ultrasound scan performed between 18 and 22 weeks of gestation is appropriate in all pregnancies as a part of routine antenatal evaluation and screening because it provides the pregnant woman and her care provider with information about multiple aspects of her pregnancy. The obstetrical ultrasound will inform and/or confirm the number of fetuses present, the gestational age, and the location of the placenta. It will present an opportunity to diagnose congenital anomalies, detect soft markers of aneuploidy, and identify maternal pelvic pathology. [8]
Document [26] (Title: Sonography 2nd Trimester Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Introduction) Antenatal ultrasonography is widely used in pregnancy to assess fetal growth and anatomy. Although ultrasound screening is now an integral part of routine antenatal care, recommendations for the delivery of obstetric ultrasound vary from country to country. [1] [2] The history of sonography in obstetrics dates from the classic 1958 Lancet paper of Ian Donald and his team from Glasgow. Clinical researchers have seized technological developments such as real-time imaging, color and power Doppler, transvaginal sonography, and 3D and 4D imaging to enhance the investigation and management of patients in areas as diverse as assessment of fetal growth and well-being, screening for fetal anomalies, and ultrasound-guided procedures as an essential component of fetal therapy. [3]
Document [27] (Title: Sonography 3rd Trimester and Placenta Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Indications) A particular patient population that is especially benefited from using ultrasound in the third trimester is the late presenter without prior prenatal care. Unfortunately, these patients are prone to all aforementioned disorders, and an ultrasonographic evaluation is of paramount importance to avoid, or at least plan for, significant morbidity and mortality causes.

This leads to improved patient outcomes, safety, and team performance while respecting the unique challenges and complexities of obstetric care.
Document [29] (Title: Sonography Assessment of Gestational Age -- Technique or Treatment -- Second Trimester) Crown-rump length:  Crown-rump length can be measured up to about 14 weeks of gestation.  Between weeks 12 through 14, this measurement is equivalent to biparietal measurements. [10] Once the size of the crown-rump length reaches 84 mm, then the biparietal method should be used. [2] The method of measuring the crown-rump length in the second trimester is the same as described for the first trimester. The accuracy during this time period is less and can estimate gestational age accurately to within 8 days.
Document [30] (Title: Antepartum Care in the Second and Third Trimester -- Clinical Significance) Antepartum care is the health care provided during pregnancy to optimize outcomes for both the mother and the fetus. The primary objectives are to identify high-risk pregnancies and to monitor the health of the mother and the development of the fetus. Prenatal visits are an intricate balance of maternal and fetal management aimed to prevent significant maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality and provide support throughout the prenatal course. Close follow-up with timely review of new complaints or issues, physical exam findings, sonography abnormalities, and laboratory results facilitate the necessary interventions. These may include escalation of care to more frequent antepartum care visits, close follow-up by maternal-fetal medicine specialists, antepartum fetal surveillance, and potential early delivery depending on the gestational age, clinical picture, and potential improvement of outcomes.

Continuous training and skill development are essential to maintain competency in this rapidly evolving field.

Document [0] (Title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2308) Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is categorized as several forms of multiple gestation, including twin pregnancy (both delivered and unspecified for a care episode), triplet pregnancy delivered, and quadruplet pregnancy delivered as well as quadruplet pregnancy unspecified for a care episode (noting two similar Arabic expressions for the unspecified category). These specific multiple‑pregnancy classifications are considered instances of “حمل متعدد” (multiple pregnancy), which itself is included in the Standardized MedDRA Query “مضاعفات وعوامل خطر الحمل والمخاض والولادة (باستثناء حالات الإجهاض وولادة جنين ميت) (SMQ)”, a grouping that captures complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor, and delivery while excluding miscarriage and stillbirth. The SMQ, in turn, is part of the broader category “نواتج متبقية من الحمل” (remaining pregnancy outcomes). Consequently, each listed multiple‑pregnancy type—twin, triplet, and quadruplet pregnancies, whether delivered or unspecified for a care episode—is linked to the same complication‑risk SMQ, situating them within the overall framework of residual pregnancy outcomes and informing clinical assessment and management of normal pregnancy and delivery scenarios that involve multiple fetuses.

It is classified as “حمل بأربعة توائم، تمت الولادة” (four‑fetus pregnancy that has been delivered), “حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية” and “حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محددة لنوبة الرعاية” (four‑fetus pregnancies with care episodes that are not specified), “حمل بثلاثة توائم، تمت الولادة” (triplet pregnancy that has been delivered), “حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة” (twin pregnancy that has been delivered), and “حمل توأمي، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية” (twin pregnancy with an unspecified care episode). In addition, Normal pregnancy and/or delivery has the child entity “حمل متعدد” (multiple pregnancy). All of these entities—حمل متعدد, حمل بأربعة توائم، تمت الولادة, حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية, حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محددة لنوبة الرعاية, حمل بثلاثة توائم، تمت الولادة, حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة, and حمل توأمي، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية—are members of the SMQ “مضاعفات وعوامل خطر الحمل والمخاض والولادة (باستثناء حالات الإجهاض وولادة جنين ميت)” (Complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor, and delivery, excluding miscarriage and stillbirth). This grouping highlights that multiple gestations, whether resulting in delivery or recorded without a defined care episode, are recognized as clinical scenarios associated with increased obstetric complications and risk factors, informing patient assessment and management within the broader context of normal pregnancy and delivery.
Document [12] (Title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2265) Maternal and fetal conditions affecting labor and delivery comprise a broad spectrum of obstetric scenarios that influence how birth is managed. The category includes routine situations such as a normal pregnancy and spontaneous vaginal delivery, as well as standard delivery procedures that are applied when labor progresses without complication. It also encompasses a variety of multiple‑gestation pregnancies—twin (حمل توأمي), triplet (حمل بثلاثة توائم), quadruplet (حمل بأربعة توائم), and even septuplet (Septuplet pregnancy) pregnancies—each presenting unique challenges for monitoring, timing of delivery, and neonatal care. Fetal malpresentation is another important element within this group; examples include poor fetal positioning (سوء تموضع الجنين) and breech presentation that has been successfully converted to a cephalic presentation, illustrating how obstetric interventions can correct unfavorable fetal positions to facilitate a safer vaginal birth. Together, these conditions highlight the need for individualized assessment and tailored management strategies to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes during labor and delivery.
Document [13] (Title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 115) “مرحلة أولى مطولة من المخاض”, prolonged second‑stage labor “مرحلة ثانية مطولة من المخاض”, oligohydramnios “قلة السائل الأمنيوسي”, amniotic fluid embolism “انْصِمامٌ بالسَّائِلِ السَّلَوِيّ”, and the need for assisted delivery techniques. The SMQ “Complications and Risk Factors of Pregnancy, Labor, and Delivery (excluding miscarriage and stillbirth)” aggregates risk factors and complications such as precursors of pre‑eclampsia, pre‑eclampsia “تسمم الحمل”, malignant uterine fibroid “رحى عدارية خبيثة”, abdominal pregnancy “حمل بطني”, prolonged pregnancy “حمل مطول”, tubal pregnancy “حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ”, early membrane rupture “تمزق الأغشية الباكر”, abnormal pregnancy “حمل منتبذ”, mild hyperemesis gravidarum “قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة”, postpartum hemorrhage “نزف ما بعد الولادة”, retained products of conception “نواتج متبقية من الحمل”, multiple gestations (twin, triplet, quadruplet pregnancies, both delivered and not yet assigned to a care episode), failure to induce labor “فشل تحريض المخاض”,
Document [14] (Title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 905) Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is categorized into several specific multiple‑gestation types, including twin pregnancy (both delivered and unspecified for a care episode), triplet pregnancy (delivered), and quadruplet pregnancy (delivered, unspecified for a care episode, and unspecified for a care episode in a slightly different wording). These multiple‑gestation categories are considered children of the broader concept “حمل متعدد” (multiple pregnancy). All of the listed multiple‑gestation types—twin, triplet, and quadruplet pregnancies—are members of the standardized medical query “مضاعفات وعوامل خطر الحمل والمخاض والولادة (باستثناء حالات الإجهاض وولادة جنين ميت) (SMQ)”, which groups complications and risk factors related to pregnancy, labor, and delivery while excluding miscarriage and stillbirth. In addition, the conditions “تعسر المخاض” (difficult labor) and “رأب عنق الرحم” (cervical insufficiency) are also members of this same SMQ, indicating that they are recognized as important obstetric risk factors. Postpartum obstetric operation is linked to “Zorg en onderzoek onmiddellijk na bevalling” (care and investigation immediately after delivery), reflecting that any obstetric surgical intervention after birth requires prompt postpartum assessment and monitoring. Likewise, the relationship between “Normal pregnancy and/or delivery” and “Zorg en onderzoek onmiddellijk na bevalling” underscores that even uncomplicated deliveries are associated with immediate postpartum care and investigation. Together, these relationships illustrate how various forms of multiple pregnancy, specific obstetric complications, and postpartum procedures are integrated within a unified framework of pregnancy‑related risk assessment and immediate post‑delivery management.
Document [15] (Title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2278) Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is categorized under several multiple‑gestation pregnancy types. It is classified as a twin pregnancy that has resulted in delivery (حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة) as well as a twin pregnancy for which a specific care episode is not defined (حمل توأمي، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية). It is also classified as a triplet pregnancy that has resulted in delivery (حمل بثلاثة توائم، تمت الولادة). In addition, it is classified as a quadruplet pregnancy for which a specific care episode is not defined (حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية), with duplicate wording for this category resolved as a single classification. Furthermore, Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is linked to the broader concept of multiple pregnancy (حمل متعدد) as its child entity, indicating that it encompasses all forms of pregnancies involving more than one fetus. This hierarchical and categorical organization helps clinicians identify the specific gestational context—twin, triplet, or quadruplet—and whether delivery has occurred or care details remain unspecified, thereby guiding appropriate monitoring and management strategies.
Document [16] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2917) Cervical shortening, second trimester, is classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that this cervical change occurring in the second trimester is considered a sign of early or threatened labor.
Document [17] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2402) Delivery of vertex presentation refers to a vaginal delivery of the fetus when the baby is positioned head‑first, representing a specific type of vaginal delivery.
Document [18] (Title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2042) MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is a broad clinical category that includes various forms of multiple‑gestation pregnancy. Within this category are specific conditions identified in different languages, such as the Czech term těhotenství s paterčaty, the Dutch phrase zwangerschap; zesling, and the Arabic expressions حمل توأمي (twin pregnancy), حمل بثلاثة توائم (triplet pregnancy), and حمل بأربعة توائم (quadruplet pregnancy). These multiple‑gestation pregnancies are recognized as distinct maternal and fetal conditions because they can significantly influence labor and delivery management, requiring specialized monitoring and obstetric care to address the increased risks associated with carrying two, three, four, or more fetuses.
Document [19] (Title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2080) Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is recognized as a clinical category that can be classified in several ways. It is identified as “Zwangerschap als bijkomstige bevinding,” meaning pregnancy as an incidental finding. In addition, it is classified as a multiple‑gestation pregnancy in various specific scenarios: a twin pregnancy that has resulted in delivery (“حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة”), a twin pregnancy that is not specified for a care episode (“حمل توأمي، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية”), a triplet pregnancy that has resulted in delivery (“حمل بثلاثة توائم، تمت الولادة”), and a quadruplet pregnancy that is not specified for a care episode (recorded in Arabic as both “حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية” and “حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محددة لنوبة الرعاية”). These classifications illustrate that normal pregnancy and/or delivery encompasses both uncomplicated singleton outcomes and a range of multiple‑gestation outcomes, each of which may be documented as either completed deliveries or as cases without a defined care episode.
Document [20] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2370) Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is recognized as a pregnancy that can appear as an incidental finding (“Zwangerschap als bijkomstige bevinding”) and is classified into several multiple‑gestation categories. It is classified as “حمل توأمي، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية” (twin pregnancy, care episode not specified), as “حمل بثلاثة توائم، تمت الولادة” (triplet pregnancy, delivery completed), and as “حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية” (quadruplet pregnancy, care episode not specified). This illustrates that the concept of normal pregnancy and/or delivery encompasses a range of multiple‑birth scenarios, each with distinct documentation regarding the stage of care.
Document [21] (Title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2118) Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is classified as a pregnancy with four fetuses (حمل بأربعة توائم) for which the care episode is unspecified (غير محدد/غير محددة لنوبة الرعاية), and it is also classified as a twin pregnancy that has already resulted in delivery (حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة). This indicates that the term “Normal pregnancy and/or delivery” can encompass multiple‑fetus gestations, including both quadruplet pregnancies with undefined care episodes and twin pregnancies that have reached the delivery stage.

Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 1991) Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is categorized under several specific obstetric classifications. It is classified as حمل بأربعة توائم، تمت الولادة (a quadruple pregnancy that has resulted in delivery) and also as حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محددة لنوبة الرعاية (a quadruple pregnancy without a defined care episode). In addition, it is classified as حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة (a twin pregnancy that has resulted in delivery) and as حمل توأمي، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية (a twin pregnancy without a defined care episode). Moreover, Normal pregnancy and/or delivery has a child relationship with حمل متعدد (multiple pregnancy), indicating that multiple gestations—including twin and quadruple pregnancies—are encompassed within the broader concept of normal pregnancy and delivery. These classifications enable clinicians to distinguish between delivered and undetermined care episodes for twin and quadruple gestations, facilitating accurate documentation and appropriate management in obstetric care.
Document [24] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431) Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.
Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 806) Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is recognized as a clinical condition that can be documented as a multiple‑gestation pregnancy. It is classified as a quadruple pregnancy that has resulted in delivery (حمل بأربعة توائم، تمت الولادة), as a quadruple pregnancy with an unspecified care episode (حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية / حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محددة لنوبة الرعاية), as a triple pregnancy that has resulted in delivery (حمل بثلاثة توائم، تمت الولادة), as a twin pregnancy that has resulted in delivery (حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة), and as a twin pregnancy with an unspecified care episode (حمل توأمي، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية). In addition, Normal pregnancy and/or delivery has a child entity denoted as multiple pregnancy (حمل متعدد). The condition is also linked to the finding of pregnancy as an incidental observation (Zwangerschap als bijkomstige bevinding). Immediate postpartum care and investigation (Zorg en onderzoek onmiddellijk na bevalling) is classified in relation to Normal pregnancy and/or delivery, indicating that patients with this condition require prompt evaluation after birth. Postpartum obstetric operation likewise has a relationship with immediate postpartum care and investigation, reflecting that surgical interventions after delivery are managed within the same immediate postpartum care framework.
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 797) Normal pregnancy and delivery are classified into several multiple‑gestation categories—twin pregnancy delivered, twin pregnancy unspecified for care, triplet pregnancy delivered, quadruplet pregnancy delivered, and quadruplet pregnancy unspecified for care (both “غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية” and “غير محددة لنوبة الرعاية”)—which are members of the broader SMQ “مضاعفات وعوامل خطر الحمل والمخاض والولادة (باستثناء حالات الإجهاض وولادة جنين ميت)”. Additional obstetric conditions belonging to this SMQ include cervical dystocia (“عسر ولادة متعلق بعنق الرحم”), prolonged labor (“مخاض طويل”), induced labor (“مخاض محفز”), postpartum sepsis (“إنتان الدم بعد الولادة”), retained placenta or membranes without hemorrhage (“انحباس المشيمة أو الأغشية، من دون نزف”), other postpartum hemorrhage (“رضح توليدي آخر، غير ذلك”), fetal malpresentation (“سوء تموضع الجنين”), malignant tumor of the placenta (ورم خبيث في المشيمة), breech presentation without mention of rotation (وضعية المَجيء المقعدي من دون ذكر الانقلاب), and uterine atony (ونی الرحم). Collectively, these entities represent recognized pregnancy‑related complications and risk factors—excluding miscarriage and stillbirth—and are used by clinicians to assess maternal‑fetal risk, guide prenatal monitoring, and plan preventive or therapeutic interventions during labor and delivery.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2230) Medical databases are clinically associated with a variety of obstetric and perinatal conditions. By linking these entities, databases support the identification, monitoring, and management of these complications, facilitating evidence‑based decision‑making in maternal‑fetal medicine.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1053) Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is a broad diagnostic category that includes COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY. Within this category, a specific manifestation is Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester, which is further classified as Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage. Recognizing that spotting in an unspecified trimester represents an early‑trimester hemorrhagic event and is considered a pregnancy‑related complication helps clinicians organize maternal conditions that affect pregnancy, delivery, or the puerperium and guides appropriate assessment and management.
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 814) Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is classified as several specific forms of multiple pregnancy, including حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة (twin pregnancy with delivery), حمل توأمي، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية (twin pregnancy unspecified for a care episode), حمل بثلاثة توائم، تمت الولادة (triplet pregnancy with delivery), حمل بأربعة توائم، تمت الولادة (quadruplet pregnancy with delivery), حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية (quadruplet pregnancy unspecified for a care episode), and حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محددة لنوبة الرعاية (quadruplet pregnancy unspecified for a care episode). These specific pregnancy types are all children of the broader category حمل متعدد (multiple pregnancy). Both حمل متعدد and each of its constituent categories are members of مضاعفات وعوامل خطر الحمل والمخاض والولادة (باستثناء حالات الإجهاض وولادة جنين ميت) (SMQ), the standardized query for complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor, and delivery that excludes miscarriage and stillbirth. This hierarchical classification links normal pregnancy and delivery to various multiple‑gestation scenarios and situates them within a recognized risk‑factor framework, facilitating comprehensive risk assessment and targeted clinical management for patients experiencing twin, triplet, or quadruplet pregnancies.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491) Medical databases serve as clinically relevant resources for a range of obstetric conditions. They are linked to the study and management of الْتِهابُ المَشيماءِ والسَّلَى (inflammation of the placenta and membranes), حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ (a specific type of abnormal pregnancy), قلة السائل الأمنيوسي (oligohydramnios), نواتج متبقية من الحمل (retained products of conception), and gestation twin (twin pregnancy). By aggregating data on these entities, databases support diagnosis, risk assessment, and therapeutic decision‑making in maternal‑fetal medicine.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 528) Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is classified under several multiple‑gestation categories, including twin pregnancy (both delivered and unspecified for a care episode), triplet pregnancy delivered, and quadruplet pregnancy (delivered, unspecified for a care episode, and unspecified for a care episode with a slightly different wording). These specific multiple‑pregnancy types, together with the broader category “multiple pregnancy,” are all considered members of the SMQ titled “Complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor and delivery (excluding miscarriage and stillbirth).” In addition to the multiple‑gestation categories, other obstetric conditions such as cervical dystocia (difficult labor related to the cervical neck), prolonged labor, induced labor, and placental or membrane entrapment without hemorrhage are also members of this same SMQ. The SMQ itself is linked to the obstetric presentations of seated fetal position without mention of inversion and to uterine atony. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that normal pregnancy and delivery can be contextualized within a spectrum of multiple‑gestation classifications and that all of these gestational and labor conditions are grouped under a unified framework of pregnancy‑related complications and risk factors, highlighting their interconnections for clinical assessment and patient management.
